svg.text: VIETNAMESE
QUOC NGU SCRIPT
QUOC NGU — "NATIONAL LANGUAGE" SCRIPT
Vietnamese uses Latin alphabet; developed by Alexandre de Rhodes (~1651)
Catholic missionary designed system to represent Vietnamese sounds
29 letters; 5 diacritics for modified vowels; 6 tone marks
MODIFIED VOWEL LETTERS
ă /ɐ/ (short a) • â /ɐ:/ (long back a) • đ /d/ (implosive d)
ê /e/ (closed e) • ô /o/ (closed o) • ơ /ɔ:/ (open o)
ư /ɯ/ (unrounded u)
SIX TONE DIACRITICS
Level a (no mark) • Falling à • Rising á
Broken-falling ả • Rising-stopped ã • Short-stopped ạ
Diacritics stack: chẩng = tone mark on modified vowel
SAMPLE TEXT
Xin chào! "Hello!" (lit. "please greet")
Cảm ơn. "Thank you."
ZHUANG · SAWCUENGH · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ZHUANG WRITING HISTORY
Sawndip: classical script using Chinese characters for Zhuang
~1000+ years of Sawndip use; each character = Zhuang word/syllable
1955: Latin Sawcuengh alphabet created for Zhuang (PRC policy)
Sawcuengh: tone suffixes instead of diacritics (q x j b h z)
SAWCUENGH TONE ENCODING
Tone 1 (level): no suffix • Tone 2: z • Tone 3: j
Tone 4: x • Tone 5: q • Tone 6: h • Tone 8: (final p/t/k)
Tone suffix at END of syllable (not above vowel like Vietnamese)
SCRIPT COMPARISON
Both: Latin-based scripts for tonal languages
Vietnamese: diacritics ABOVE vowels • Zhuang: tone letters AT END
Vietnamese: more letters per syllable • Zhuang: cleaner syllables
Both had Chinese-character scripts before Latin: chu Nom / Sawndip
PLATE 38a ALT · VIETNAMESE / ZHUANG · SCRIPT
